result:
If I could give one piece of unsolicited advice to my younger self, it would be this: be less afraid of the quiet. I spent so many years fleeing from it, stuffing every silence with music, podcasts, the phantom buzz of a phone in my hand. I believed that an empty moment was a wasted one, a blank space to be filled before the dread of my own thoughts could seep in.

But the quiet isn't empty. It’s full. It’s the deep hum of the refrigerator, the distant sigh of a car on a wet road, the precise click of a branch against the windowpane. It’s the space where your own breath becomes a tide, and your mind, no longer a frantic squirrel, begins to stretch out like a cat in a sunbeam. Thoughts you’ve been chasing finally turn around and come to you.

I found the quiet by accident, on a walk with a dead phone. At first, it was unnerving—just me and the rhythmic crunch of my feet on gravel. But then, the world began to perform its subtle, magnificent show. A hawk’s shadow sliding over the field, the architecture of a bare winter tree against a gray sky, the cold, clean scent of approaching snow. I wasn't just looking at the world; I was listening to it, and in doing so, I finally heard myself.

Now, I court the quiet. I leave my headphones at home. I sit with a cup of tea and just watch the light change on the wall. In those still pockets, I am not planning or regretting. I am simply being, a creature among other creatures, present in the tender, unadorned miracle of an ordinary afternoon. The quiet, I’ve learned, is not a void to fear, but a sanctuary to return to, again and again.
